title: Paget Disease of Bone: Diagnosis, Management, and Clinical Cases

items.question: On the basis of this patient's clinical presentation, which of the following is the best next step in his management?
options: B. Alendronate, 40 mg daily for 6 months
E. Reassurance, as no additional workup or treatment is indicated
items.explanation: The patient has biochemical and radiographic evidence for PDB but is asymptomatic. Whole-body bone scanning with technetium $^{99}$Tc (Answer C) is indicated to assess the extent of disease, especially considering potential multifocal involvement.
items.reference: CASE 1: "On the basis of this patient's clinical presentation, which of the following is the best next step in his management?"

items.question: How does bisphosphonate treatment affect patients with painful Paget disease of bone?
items.answer: Bisphosphonates, particularly intravenous forms like zoledronic acid, are effective in reducing skeletal pain and have a greater sustained effect compared to oral therapy.
keyPoints: Painful PDB is responsive to bisphosphonate treatment.
Intravenous bisphosphonates confer a greater and more sustained effect than oral forms.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Painful PDB is responsive to treatment with bisphosphonates, with a greater and more sustained effect conferred by intravenous than oral therapy."

items.question: Why is expectant management advised for patients with asymptomatic Paget disease of bone?
items.answer: There is insufficient evidence that pharmacologic interventions, including bisphosphonates, provide benefits such as prevention of deformity or fractures in asymptomatic PDB.
keyPoints: Lack of evidence supporting the benefit of treatment for asymptomatic PDB.
Expectant management and symptomatic treatment are currently advised.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Importantly, there is insufficient evidence of other disease benefit (ie, prevention of deformity, fractures, orthopedic surgery, hearing loss) with bisphosphonates or other pharmacologic/nonpharmacologic interventions, such that expectant management and treatment of patients with PDB who have been pain is currently advised."
